tickets.title: US001
tickets.description:
As a Cafeteria Chef, I want to instantly mark a specific menu item as Sold Out on the kitchen display screen, so that students are immediately prevented from ordering items that are out of stock.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Pass/Fail Check 1: Verify that a prominent "Mark as Sold Out" toggle button is available next to each menu item card on the kitchen display UI.
- [ ] Pass/Fail Check 2: Verify that toggling an item to "Sold Out" changes its visual background state to grey and immediately upda

tickets.title: US004
tickets.description:
As a Student, I want to receive a push notification at 11:30 AM detailing a discount on the daily special menu item, so that I can save money on my lunch order.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Pass/Fail Check 1: Verify that the automated background notification engine pushes a promotional alert text precisely at 11:30 AM server-time to opted-in student devices.
- [ ] Pass/Fail Check 2: Verify that clicking the received push notification routes the student directly to the specific daily special item

analysis.ambiguity1: The Marketing Department's request to "send push notifications to students at 11:30 AM offering them a discount" is ambiguous regarding its target audience scope. It does not clarify whether the notification should be blasted globally to all registered student accounts, or filtered based on real-time geo-location proximity to the cafeteria and historical purchasing behavior. Additionally, it lacks parameters on user preference configurations to prevent spamming students who opt out of promotional messages.
analysis.ambiguity2: The Chef's requirement to "mark an item as Sold Out instantly" is ambiguous regarding concurrency and active shopping sessions. The system does not specify how to handle transactional edge cases where a student has already added the item (e.g., the vegan wrap) to their shopping cart and is mid-checkout at the exact millisecond the Chef toggles the status to sold out. It needs to define whether the system should forcefully eject the item from active carts or trigger a custom real-time inventory validation error during payment.
analysis.conflictSolution:
Conflict: A technical operational conflict exists between the Cafeteria Manager's goal of allowing continuous mobile student ordering during walk-time and the IT Team's constraint that the kitchen display operates offline for 10 minutes due to terrible basement Wi-Fi. If students place orders via the cloud while the kitchen display is disconnected, it causes data asymmetry, leading to delayed preparation, incorrect queuing, and potential order loss.

Resolution: Implement an asynchronous data architecture utilizing local caching (such as IndexedDB or LocalStorage) on the kitchen display hardware client. When a Wi-Fi dropout occurs, the kitchen display must queue incoming orders locally and allow chefs to update item availability offline. Concurrently, the student-facing app will mark orders placed during this window as "Pending Kitchen Confirmation". Upon connection restoration within the 10-minute threshold, a two-way background synchronization protocol will execute, reconciling the order queue and inventory statuses using strict chronological timestamps.
analysis.nfr1: Offline Operational Resiliency: The Kitchen Display System (KDS) must remain fully operational and interactable in an offline state for a minimum duration of 10 continuous minutes without application crashes or internal memory leaks during local network or Wi-Fi disruptions.
analysis.nfr2: Allergen Exclusion Filtering: The customer-facing user interface must render an instantaneous, error-free menu filtration mechanism that entirely hides all items containing specified allergens (e.g., peanuts) from the screen based on verified user profile preferences.
analysis.nfr3: Real-time Inventory Propagation: Status modifications executed by kitchen staff (such as marking a menu item as "Sold Out") must propagate and update across all active client-side mobile student application interfaces within a maximum latency threshold of 2 seconds.
